coredata.dc:title: 3D printed cinnamon essential oil/banana peel carbon dots loaded corn starch/gelatin bilayer film with enhanced functionality for food packaging application
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Kai
Zhang, Min
Bhandari, Bhesh
Deng, Dewei
coredata.dc:description: Using 3D printing technology, a gelatin-polyvinyl alcohol‑carbon dots (GPC) layer+corn starch-polyvinyl alcohol-cinnamon essential oil (CPC) layer active bilayer film with an external barrier function and an internal controlled-release effect was successfully produced for food preservation. The GPC film was provided with potent antioxidant and UV blocking properties by the banana peel carbon dots (CDs). The cinnamon essential oil (CEO) had the strongest interaction with the film matrix at 3% (w/w), causing the CPC film having the lowest surface wettability, good integrity, and lowest crystallinity. The CEO's stability and releasing effectiveness were greatly enhanced by the creation of a bilayer film. At 60% filling rate of the CPC layer, the bilayer film showed the highest CEO retention after drying and the best CEO release performance. Finally, the created active bilayer film was found to significantly improve the sensory quality stability of the spicy essential oil microcapsule powders. It also successfully extended the mangoes' shelf life by delaying browning and rot.

Cinnamon essential oil

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Using 3D printing technology, a gelatin-polyvinyl alcohol‑carbon dots (GPC) layer+corn starch-polyvinyl alcohol-cinnamon essential oil (CPC) layer active bilayer film with an external barrier function and an internal controlled-release effect was successfully produced for food preservation. The GPC film was provided with potent antioxidant and UV blocking properties by the banana peel carbon dots (CDs). The cinnamon essential oil (CEO) had the strongest interaction with the film matrix at 3% (w/w), causing the CPC film having the lowest surface wettability, good integrity, and lowest crystallinity. The CEO's stability and releasing effectiveness were greatly enhanced by the creation of a bilayer film. At 60% filling rate of the CPC layer, the bilayer film showed the highest CEO retention after drying and the best CEO release performance. Finally, the created active bilayer film was found to significantly improve the sensory quality stability of the spicy essential oil microcapsule powders. It also successfully extended the mangoes' shelf life by delaying browning and rot.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Corn starch-polyvinyl alcohol-cinnamon essential oil composite material
Corn starch-polyvinyl alcohol composite material loaded with 1% cinnamon essential oil
Corn starch-polyvinyl alcohol composite material loaded with 2% cinnamon essential oil
Corn starch-polyvinyl alcohol composite material loaded with 3% cinnamon essential oil
Corn starch-polyvinyl alcohol composite material loaded with 4% cinnamon essential oil
Corn starch-polyvinyl alcohol composite material loaded with 5% cinnamon essential oil

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Most of food products deterioration occurs during transportation and storage. It is a key challenge for food industry to effectively preserve the quality and extend the shelf life of food products. When it comes to food preservation, cost-effective packaging film is one of the primary strategies. Owing to the adverse impacts of nonbiodegradable petroleum-based packaging materials on the human health and environment, the naturally occurring biopolymer materials have been increasingly developed and utilized because of their safety, degradability, and environmental friendliness (Ansarifar & Moradinezhad, 2022; Tian, Yan, Rajulu, Xiang, & Luo, 2017; Zhou, Fu, Bian, Chang, & Zhang, 2022). Structuring biopolymers like proteins (e.g., gelatin, GT) and polysaccharides (e.g., corn starch, CS) are promising materials for biodegradable packaging films fabrication owing to their ideal functional properties such as: film-forming ability, transparency, flexibility, and barrier ability against UV light and oxygen (Chen et al., 2023; Hosseini, Ghaderi, & Gómez-Guillén, 2021). However, these materials have the defects of low mechanical strength and poor stability against water, which limit their commercial application. Blends of these materials with synthetic polymers like polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) are more desirable to enhance the comprehensive performance of the resulting films (Kaynarca, Kamer, Gumus, & Sagdıc, 2023).

As a physical barrier, the packaging film can create a micro-environment for food products, which is beneficial to slow down their biological and chemical reactions. Nevertheless, the quality-keeping capacity of a single package is limit. To strengthen the antimicrobial and antioxidant effects of the packaging films, various active ingredients have been incorporated into the film matrix. In recent years, essential oils have gained a great deal of attentions and preferences in field of packaging material due to their potent bioactivity, natural image, non-toxicity, and biodegradability (Sharma et al., 2023; Wang et al., 2022). Cinnamon essential oil (CEO) is among the most popular essential oil with strong and broad-spectrum antimicrobial properties. For example, CEO was loaded in chitosan/gelatin film for antimicrobial packaging preparation (Fan et al., 2023). Guan et al. (2024) fabricated the CEO-loaded nanofiber active film by uniaxial electrospinning technique, which prolonged the shelf life of griskin by two days. Moreover, CEO-encapsulated hybrid wax coatings were applied for enhancing apple safety against food borne pathogens (Arcot et al., 2024).

The antioxidant and antimicrobial effects of the active films are highly correlated with the loaded active substances which can be retained at a certain effective dose for a long time (Yang et al., 2022). However, the homogeneous distribution of essential oil in the single-layer active packaging film makes its release into the packaging and external environment simultaneously, which causes a rapid loss and substantial waste of essential oil. Additionally, the essential oil is easy to oxidize and degrade under the action of environmental factors such as oxygen, UV light, high temperature, etc., resulting in a significant loss of its activity (Cossetin et al., 2021; Turek & Stintzing, 2013). Therefore, it is important to develop a new type of active packaging film that provides stable and targeted release of essential oil in the film.

The bilayer film can overly a barrier layer outside the essential oil layer, reducing the ineffective release of essential oil, and can further improved the oxygen and UV barrier ability of the film by adding functional components. Carbon dots (CDs) are a new type of carbon nanomaterials with strong antioxidant and antimicrobial activities as well as strong UV blocking ability (Zhao, Zhang, Mujumdar, Adhikari, & Wang, 2022). Additionally, due to their broad source, non/low toxicity, and good biocompatibility, these materials have gradually been used in the fields of food packaging and food preservation (Zhao, Zhang, Mujumdar, & Wang, 2023). Thus, introducing carbon dots into the barrier layer can not only protect the essential oil in the film, but also work together with the essential oil to achieve the stronger stability of food.

Further, the construction of bilayer film makes the structure of inner layer have a greater design space. Three-dimensional (3D) printing is an emerging manufacturing technology that builds objects through layer-by-layer deposition (Teng, Zhang, & Mujumdar, 2021). The technical characteristics of 3D printing make it possible for the rapid one step-molding and structure design of bilayer packaging film. It not only has faster film construction speed than the conventional solution casting method, but also can print the film into the designed shape, structure, and porosity (Guo, Zhang, & Bhandari, 2019; Li et al., 2022), thereby enabling to regulate the release of active compounds and realize its maximum utilization. However, as far as we know, there is no study on the construction of active bilayer food packaging film using 3D printing technology.

Therefore, the purpose of present study was to develop an active bilayer film with external barrier and internal controlled-release effects based on 3D printing technology for food preservation. To achieve this, the effect of PVA, CEO, and CDs addition on the properties of films were studied. Additionally, the effects of filling rate of CPC layer (inner layer) on the functionalities of bilayer films were investigated. Ultimately, two application scenarios—mango and spicy essential oil microcapsule powder—were selected to confirm the developed bilayer film's promise as an effective and high-performance food packaging film for food preservation.

Cinnamon essential oil (CEO), pepper essential oil (PEO), Sichuan pepper essential oil (SPEO), ginger essential oil (GEO), and star anise essential oil (SAEO) were kindly provided by Xuemailon Food Flavor Co., Ltd. (Zhengzhou, China). Spicy essential oil was prepared by mixing PEO, SPEO, GEO, and SAEO at a mass ratio of 11:10:1:1 according to the optimal mixing ratio from sensory evaluation. Fresh mangoes were purchased from local market (Wuxi, China), they have uniform size (with the length, width, and height of 5.4–6.1, 3.5–3.9, 2.7–3.0 cm, respectively) and maturity without mechanical damage, diseases, and pests. Corn starch (CS) was purchased from Liangrun whole grain food Co., Ltd. (Xinxiang, China). Dried banana peel was bought from Xianxiangwei (Fujian, China). Glycerin, gelatin (GT), polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) (1750 ± 50 type), gum Arabic (GA), 2,2-Diphenyl-1-picrylhydrazyl (DPPH), and 2,2′-Azinobis-(3-ethylbenzthiazoline-6-sulphonate) (ABTS) were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China) and analytically pure. Quinoa protein isolate (QPI) (purity of 90%) was bought from Tuofeng Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Xi'an, China). Escherichia coli (E. coli ATCC 43894) was acquired from Institute of Microbiology, Chinese Academy of Sciences (Beijing, China).

The CEO Pickering emulsion was prepared according to our previous study (Chen, Zhang, Wang, Mujumdar, & Deng, 2024). The obtained emulsion contained 10% (w/w) CEO and 5% QPI-GA conjugates (prepared by ultrasound-assisted wet heating).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of banana peel carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: Following the method of Yu, Zhang, Mujumdar, and Huang (2023), the dried banana peel was pulverized using a pulverizer and passed through a 100-mesh sieve to obtain banana peel powders. The powders were added to distilled water at a ratio of 1:25 and mixed well to obtain a homogenate. The homogenate was loaded into a 50 mL polytetrafluoroethylene-lined hydrothermal reactor and heated in a muffle furnace at 200 °C for 2 h. After the reactor cooled down, the reacted product was taken out and centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min to obtain a brown supernatant. The supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane filter and transferred to a dialysis bag with a cut-off molecular weight of 500 Da. After 48 h of dialysis, the liquid in the bag was lyophilized to obtain carbon dots (CDs). The resulting CDs powder were sealed in a plastic bag and stored at 4 °C.
The active bilayer film consisted of a GT-PVA-CDs (GPC) layer (barrier layer) and a CS-PVA-CEO (CPC) layer (active layer).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of film material of barrier layer
ce:section.ce:para: PVA was added to distilled water and stirred at 95 °C to form a PVA solution (6%, w/w). Then, 20 g of GT was added to 80 mL of PVA solution, while 6.2 g glycerol (25% of the total dry weight of GT and PVA) was added as a plasticizer. The mixed solution was stirred at 60 °C, and after GT was completely dissolved, the CDs solution was added to obtain the film materials of barrier layer (containing 0.5% (w/w) CDs), which was named as GPC. The composite material of GT and PVA (without CDs) was named as GP. In the optimization test of PVA addition, the concentration of PVA solution was set to 0%, 6%, 9%, and 12% (w/w), respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of film material of active layer
ce:section.ce:para: Twelve grams of CS, 88 mL of PVA solution (7%, w/w), 4.92 g of glycerol, and 1.82–9.10 mL of CEO Pickering emulsion were mixed in 500 mL beakers and replenished to 120 g with distilled water. After sealing the mouth of the beaker with plastic wrap, the mixed solution was stirred at 80 °C for 20 min to completely paste the CS. In the obtained film materials, the theoretical content of CEO was 1%, 2%, 3%, 4%, and 5% of the total dry weight of CS and PVA, thus they were named as CP-1%CEO, CP-2%CEO, CP-3%CEO, CP-4%CEO, and CP-5%CEO, respectively. The composite material of CS and PVA (without CEO) was named as CP. It was determined by pre-experimentation that the film material required to print active layers (50 × 50 mm2) with 20%, 40%, 60%, 80%, and 100% filling rates were 0.577 ± 0.021, 1.050 ± 0.017, 1.530 ± 0.030, 1.990 ± 0.026, and 2.540 ± 0.011 g, respectively, showing that the weight of the required material was highly positively correlated with the filling rate, which highlighted a good printing stability of 3D printer. In order to maintain consistent CEO amount in active layers with different filling rates, CP-1%CEO, CP-2%CEO, CP-3%CEO, CP-4%CEO, and CP-5%CEO were used as the printing inks for the active layers with 100%, 80%, 60%, 40%, and 20% filling rates, respectively. Moreover, in the optimization test of PVA addition, the concentration of PVA solution was set to 0%, 7%, 11%, and 15% (w/w), respectively.

The film materials of barrier layer and active layer were loaded into cylinders, and the 3D printer (SHINNOVE-D1, Shiyin Tech, China) was used to print the barrier layer first and then the active layer. The barrier layer material was composed of 20 g of GT, 80 mL of PVA solution (6% w/w), 6.2 g of glycerol, and 0.531 g of CDs. The printing parameters of the barrier layer were, nozzle diameter: 0.4 mm; nozzle height: 0.4 mm; printing temperature: 35–37 °C; number of printing layers: 2 layers; filling mode: cross-filling; filling rate: 100%; film size: 50 × 50 mm2 (for analysis) and 100 × 100 mm2 (for application). The active layer materials were composed of 12 g of CS, 88 mL of PVA solution (7% w/w), 4.92 g of glycerol, 1.82–9.10 mL of CEO Pickering emulsion, and 6.36–13.64 mL of distilled water. The printing parameters of the active layer were, nozzle diameter: 0.4 mm; nozzle height: 1.2 mm; printing temperature: 25 °C; number of printing layers: 2 layers; filling mode: cross-filling; filling rate: 20% ∼ 100%; film size: 50 × 50 mm2 (for analysis) and 100 × 100 mm2 (for application). After printing, the films were dried in an oven at 30 °C, and the resulting active bilayer films with active layer filling rates of 20%, 40%, 60%, 80%, and 100% were named as GPC + CPC-1, GPC + CPC-2, GPC + CPC-3, GPC + CPC-4, and GPC + CPC-5, respectively.

The rheological property analysis of film materials was performed using a hybrid rheometer (Discovery HR-3, TA Instruments, USA) equipped with a 40-mm diameter parallel plate geometry (Phuhongsung, Zhang, & Devahastin, 2020). The test spacing was set at 1000 μm. The viscosity of CS-based film materials was measured in the shear rate range of 0.1–100 s−1 at 25 °C, while their G' (storage modulus) and G" (loss modulus) were determined in the oscillation stress range of 1–1000 Pa. The G' and G" of GT-based film materials were measured in the temperature range of 25–40 °C.
The infrared spectra of films were determined by a FTIR spectrometer in ATR mode. In this case, the scan range was from 500 to 4000 cm−1 with a resolution of 4 cm−1 and a scan number of 32.

The films (30 × 30 mm2) were adhered to the surface of the sample plate. Cu-kα-ray scan was performed using an X-ray diffractometer. The measurement parameters were: instrument operating voltage of 40 kV, current of 40 mA, scan range of 5° ∼ 40°, and scan speed of 0.05°/s.

The TGA of films was conducted with a thermogravimetric analyzer (TGA2, Mettler-Toledo, Switzerland). The analysis was carried out from 25 to 500 °C at an increasing rate of 20 °C/min under the nitrogen atmosphere.

The films (4 × 4 mm2) were adhered to the sample plate posted with conductive adhesive, and then sprayed with a thin layer of gold. Subsequently, the surface micromorphology of the films was determined using a scanning electron microscope (Regulus 8100, Hitachi, Japan) at a voltage of 3 kV.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Moisture content, total soluble matter, and surface wettability determination
ce:section.ce:para:
The moisture content and total soluble matter of the films were measured according to the following methods and equations (Wu et al., 2023). The films (50 × 50 mm2) were weighed to get weight (W1) and dried at 105 °C to a constant weight (W2). The dried films were subsequently immersed in distilled water at 25 °C for 24 h. After which, the films were dried again to the constant weight (W3).
(1)
Moisture content

= (
W 1
−
W 2
/

× 100
(2)

× 100

As for the surface wettability determination of films. The films (20 × 20 mm2) were adhered to a glass slide. The slice was placed on the carrier stage of the water contact angle analyzer (OCA15EC, Dataphysics instrument, Germany) and 5 μL of distilled water was dropped to the surface of the film with a syringe. The shape of the water droplets deposited onto the surface of the films was photographed using the camera equipped with the instrument.

The average thickness of films was calculated by the thickness of six random places of the film measured using a digital vernier caliper with accuracy of 0.001 mm (CHYQFP 12.7, Henan Bangte Tools, China). As for color, the films were placed on the top of white paper and the colorimeter (CR-400, Konica-Minolta, Japan) was aimed at the film surface to obtain the values of color parameters L* (light to dark), a* (red to green), and b* (yellow to blue) by reflection.

The mechanical property of the films (10 × 40 mm2) were tested using a texture analyzer (TA-XT Plus, Stable Micro systems, UK). The test parameters were: fixture initial distance of 20 mm and stretching speed of 1 mm/s.

The pictures of bilayer films were opened by ImageJ software (National Institutes of Health, USA), followed by setting the image type to 8 bit. Then the “threshold” button was clicked to perform the porosity analysis of active layer.

The water vapor permeation amount of films was determined according to the method of Mulla, Ahmed, Vahora, and Pathania (2023) with some modifications. The films were wrapped around the mouth of 20 mL sample bottles (the mouth diameter and height were 1.8 and 5.5 cm, respectively) filled with 5 mL of distilled water. Then, the bottles were placed in a desiccator with a relative humidity of 75%, and equilibrated at 25 °C for 24 h. Finally, the bottles were weighed again to determine the difference in weight of the water before and after equilibration.

The films (10 × 40 mm2) were attached to the inner wall of a quartz cuvette, and the transmittance of the films was scanned in the range of 200–800 nm using a UV–visible spectrophotometer (UV2600, Shimadzu, Japan) with an empty cuvette as a blank control.

The films were placed in a desiccator (25 °C) with a relative humidity of 75%, and were taken every 3 days during the 15-day storage period to determine the content of CEO in the films. Specifically, the stored films were cut into pieces and transferred to the beakers containing 30 mL of ethanol. After the beaker was sealed with plastic wrap, the ethanol solution was stirred at 200 rpm for 24 h, and then centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min to obtain the supernatant. The supernatant was diluted to a suitable concentration and the absorbance was measured at 282 nm. The CEO content was quantified according to the pre-established standard curve (y = 98.236× + 0.0074, R2 = 0.9999; where x is the CEO concentration (mg/mL), y is the corresponding absorbance). The release rate of CEO was calculated by the ratio of the decreased content of CEO in the films to the original content of CEO in the film-forming materials.

The films (20 × 20 mm2) were pasted on the inner middle of petri dish lid. 20 mL of plate counting agar medium was poured into the petri dish, and 100 μL of Escherichia coli (E. coli ATCC 43894) suspension (105 CFU/mL) was applied on the surface after the medium solidified (Doi, Sae-Eaw, Suppakul, & Chompreeda, 2019). The petri dish lid was placed on top of the petri dish and the edge was sealed with a sealing film to prevent the CEO evaporation. Subsequently, the petri dishes were incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. After incubation, the colonies on plates were counted to evaluate the antimicrobial effect of films.

The films (20 × 12 mm2) were added into 2 mL of DPPH radical ethanol solution (0.004%, w/v), the absorbance of reacted solution (experimental group) was read at 517 nm after reacting at room temperature and in dark for 30 min. The control group was not added with film samples. The DPPH radical scavenging rate of films was calculated by Eq. (3):

Herein, A1 and A2 were the absorbance of control group and experimental group, respectively.

The films (20 × 12 mm2) were added into 4 mL of ABTS+ radical aqueous solution (with the absorbance of approximately 0.8 at 734 nm), and the absorbance of reacted solution (experimental group) was measured at 734 nm after reacting at room temperature and in dark for 30 min. The control group was not added with film samples. The ABTS+ radical scavenging rate of films was calculated by Eq. (4):

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of films to food products
Application of films to spicy essential oil microcapsule powders
ce:section.ce:para: The spicy essential oil microcapsules with the wall (QPI-GA coacervates)-to-core (spicy essential oil) ratio of 2:1 were prepared following our previous study (Chen, Zhang, Mujumdar, & Wang, 2023). Prior to the storage experiment, 3 g of spicy essential oil microcapsules were spread flat in the weighing bottles, followed by sealing the mouth of the bottles with films, while the unpackaged spicy essential oil microcapsules were used as control (CK). During the 28-day storage period (25 °C), 0.5 g of microcapsules were taken every 7 days for the determination of retention rates of the characteristic taste (sanshool and capsaicinoid), color (capsanthin), and aroma (linalool) substances of spicy essential oil.

The fresh mangoes were placed in plastic bowls (The diameters of the mouth and bottom of the bowl were 9.0 and 6.5 cm, respectively, and the height of the bowl was 3.5 cm) and the mouth of the bowls was sealed with films (no contact with the mangoes), while the unpackaged mangoes were used as control (CK). During the 12-day storage period (25 °C), mangoes were taken out every 2 days for freshness evaluation.

All the tests were repeated three times and the obtained results were expressed as mean ± standard deviation. SPSS 26.0 software (SPSS Inc., USA) was used for statistical analysis. Differences between data were determined using the Ducan test with a confidence interval of 95%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization study on the preparation of bilayer active films
Influence of PVA addition on the printing and film-forming properties of GT
ce:section.ce:para: The effects of different PVA additions on the printing and film-forming properties of GT are shown in Fig. S1. Addition of PVA at 6% (w/w), the GT-PVA composite material (GP) had a good printing performance as GT at the appropriate printing temperature, the printed films were uniform and flat. However, as the addition of PVA increased to 9% and 12%, GP showed a large number of air bubbles during the preparation process due to their substantially increased viscosity, and the printing performance significantly deteriorated. After drying, the edge of films added with 9% and 12% of PVA also showed deformation, seriously affecting the flatness of the film.

In situations with high humidity, film stability is ensured by good mechanical strength following water absorption. After being immersed in water for 30 s, GT film became susceptible to being torn due to water absorption and swelling of GT. Meanwhile, all three GP films had relatively strong mechanical properties and did not show any obvious rupture during the tensile process, which was consistent with the results reported by Chen, Qiang, Chen, Ren, and Zhang (2021) that PVA addition significantly improved the mechanical properties of GT film. Based on above results, 6% PVA was the optimum addition to improve the printing and film-forming properties of GT.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of PVA addition on the printing and film-forming properties of CS
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. S2, the printing of pure CS showed easy line breakage phenomenon, and the printed film was uneven. But the printing performance was significantly improved with the addition of an appropriate amount of PVA. After adding 7% of PVA, the film material printed smoothly and the printed film was uniform and flat. The reason for the above difference might be that the PVA addition inhibited the aggregation and rearrangement of starch molecules during the cooling process of the starch paste, which retarded the gelatinization and aging of starch, and thus provided the film material with better printing performance (Liu, Chen, McClements, Peng, & Jin, 2023). With the further increase of PVA addition, the viscosity of the film material gradually increased and a large number of air bubbles generated during the preparation process. When the PVA addition reached 15%, the dense air bubbles caused the printing of film material to show line breakage phenomenon again. After drying, the CS film showed obvious cracks and was easily torn. In contrast, the CS-PVA composite films (CP films) showed good mechanical properties and were not easily torn even after immersing in water for 30 s. Our finding was in accordance with the previous study on polyvinyl alcohol/starch blend film (Tian et al., 2017). Nevertheless, a large number of air bubbles appeared in the CP films with high PVA additions (11% and 15%), which negatively affected the mechanical properties of the films as the location of the air bubbles was the first to show holes and cracks in the tensile test. In summary, PVA also improved the printing and film-forming properties of CS and was optimal at an addition level of 7% for this particular formulation.

The rheological properties of printing materials are the key characteristics that affect their printing performance and the accuracy of printed products (He, Zhang, & Fang, 2020). Based on the formulations of GP and CP film materials optimized in section 3.1, the rheological properties of the film materials before and after adding CDs and CEO Pickering emulsions to each of the two were determined, and the results are depicted in Fig. S3. GT is a thermo-responsive gel, and clarifying the phase transition temperature of GT-based gels is critical to achieving their good printing performance. From Fig. S3A, the G' of GT, GP and GPC was all larger than G" at 25 °C, indicating that these film materials tended to present a solid dominated behavior at room temperature (Wang, Zhang, Mujumdar, & Li, 2023). In addition, the G' of the three film materials showed the order of GPC > GP > GT, suggesting that the addition of PVA and CDs gradually improved the self-supporting property of GT, and also made the printed GT layer having a higher load-bearing capacity for the subsequent layers. With the increase of temperature, the G' and G" of GT, GP, and GPC all gradually decreased and reached consistency (tan δ = 1) at 36.60, 36.83 and 36.94 °C, respectively. At these three temperatures, the three film materials transformed from solids to semi-solids, which enabled them to be extruded from the printing nozzle (Wang & Ajji, 2023). The above results also illustrated that the addition of PVA and CDs sequentially increased the phase transition temperature of GT, but the change was small.

Fig. S3B shows the viscosity variation curves of CS-based film materials as a function of shear rate. It can be seen that the viscosity of CS-based film materials all decreased with the increase of shear rate, demonstrating the typical shear thinning behavior. This behavior made the above materials easy to be extruded from the nozzle under shear force during 3D printing (Zhou et al., 2021). From Fig. 3C, the yield stress of CS was 251.66 Pa. After the addition of PVA, the yield stress of the film material (CP) decreased to 155.67 Pa, and fluctuated in the range of 93.45 to 183.37 Pa with the increase of CEO addition. The yield stress of the printing material was positively correlated with the pressure that needed to be provided by the printer, and the appropriate yield stress was crucial for the smooth extrusion of the printing material (Lewis, 2006). The high yield stress of CS might be responsible for its line breakage during printing (Liu, Bhandari, & Zhang, 2020) (Fig. S2).

Fig. 1A and B shows the FTIR spectra of GT-based and CS-based films. As displayed in Fig. 1A, the FTIR spectrum of GT film showed its characteristic peaks at 3291.4, 2930.0, 1632.7, 1551.0, and 1238.7 cm−1, which correspond to the amide A bond (–OH/N–H stretching vibration), amide B bond (CH stretching vibration), amide I band (CO and CN stretching vibration), amide II band (NH bending vibration), and amide III band (CN stretching vibration), respectively (Chen, Zhang, et al., 2023; Pan, Li, & Tao, 2020). With the addition of PVA (GP film), the characteristic peaks of amide A bond and amide B bond in GT blue-shifted and red-shifted, respectively, suggesting the hydrogen bonding occurred between GT and PVA molecules. The characteristic peaks of CDs appeared at 3354.7, 1602.2, and 1021.0 cm−1 corresponded to –OH, CC, and CO stretching vibration, respectively (Zhao, 2022). The spectrum of GPC film was similar to that of GP film and did not show any new absorption peaks, which might be attributed to the low addition of CDs in GPC film, and the absorption peaks of GP film covered the characteristic peaks of CDs. Xu et al. (2020) also found that the CDs addition did not form new absorption peaks in the spectrum of the composite film. However, the CDs addition further blue-shifted the characteristic peak of the –OH group of GP film to 3289.5 cm−1, indicating the further enhancement of hydrogen bonding in the composite film (GPC film).

As for CS film (Fig. 1B), the characteristic peaks at 3284.4, 2925.0, and 1015.3 cm−1 in the spectrum were attributed to the stretching vibrations of –OH, CH, and CO, whereas the characteristic peak at 1644.9 cm−1 could be generated by the bending vibration of the hydrogen bonding between the –OH in starch and the glycerol or water molecules (Gao, Hao, Cheng, Song, & Wang, 2023). The incorporation of PVA induced the obvious changes in characteristic peak positions of CS film, the characteristic peaks representing –OH and hydrogen bond blue-shifted and red-shifted to 3280.0 and 1650.2 cm−1, respectively, which could be attributed to the formation of inter- and intra-molecular hydrogen bonding between PVA and CS, resulting in the reduction of free –OH in starch molecules that could bind to glycerol or water. The characteristic peaks at 1673.7 and 1625.3 cm−1 in the CEO spectrum were generated by the CO stretching vibration. The absorption peak at 1449.7 cm−1 was a characteristic peak of aromatic hydrocarbons (C=C) in the CEO, the peaks associated to the C–OH stretching vibration in phenolics appeared at 1120.6 and 971.0 cm−1, and the absorption peaks at 745.7 and 687.4 cm−1 are related to the phenyl group of cinnamaldehyde (Wang et al., 2022). The CEO addition did not produce new peaks in the spectrum of CP film, but caused different degrees of displacement of the –OH and CH related characteristic peaks, which responded to the reduction of free –OH in the CP film and the formation of hydrogen bonds between CP and CEO molecules. Odjo et al. (2022) reported similar results and determined that the lemon oil was bonded to chitosan films through hydrogen bonds. In addition, the maximum difference in the positions of the characteristic peaks in the spectra of CP film and CP-3%CEO film indicated the strongest interaction between CP and CEO at addition of 3%.

XRD is widely used to analyze the crystalline structure of composite films and the compatibility between film materials. As presented in Fig. 4C, a diffraction peak and an amorphous broad peak appeared at 2θ = 6.8° and 20.3° in the spectrum of GT film, respectively, and both peaks were related to the triple helix structure of GT (Badii, MacNaughtan, Mitchell, & Farhat, 2014; Bigi, Panzavolta, & Rubini, 2004). The addition of PVA shifted the amorphous peak of GT from 20.3° to 19.5° (GP), and the peak intensity increased significantly. This might be due to the reason that PVA was a crystalline polymer with a strong diffraction peak at 2θ = 19.6°, and the incorporation of PVA in the GT film caused the composite film (GP film) to exhibit the crystalline characteristics of PVA (Zhao, 2022). Additionally, the peak position and intensity of GP film did not change significantly after CDs addition, indicating that the CDs did not change the crystalline structure of the composite film and had good compatibility with the film matrix.

The spectrum of CS film showed intensive diffraction peaks at 2θ = 16.8° and 19.4° (Fig. 1D), which was caused by the crystallization of straight-chain starch in the CS film and the recrystallization of branched-chain starch during the film storage process (Gao et al., 2023). The intensity of the diffraction peaks at 16.8° and 19.4° of CP film was significantly weaker than that of CS film, and tended to be amorphous after the further addition of 3% CEO. This illustrated that the hydrogen bonding between CS and PVA or CEO both significantly reduced the crystallinity of CS, thus putting the CPC film in a low crystalline state at a certain CEO addition (3%), which was consistent with the results of FTIR analysis.

As shown in Fig. 1E and G, both GT film and CS film had three main weight loss stages, presenting typical thermal degradation profiles of glycerol plasticized biomacromolecule films (Freitas, González-Martínez, & Chiralt, 2023). The first stage of weight loss occurred at 50–120 °C and was related to the volatilization of water. The second stage of weight loss occurred at approximate 120–220 °C, which was attributed to the loss of plasticizer glycerol molecules from the films. Afterwards (about 250–350 °C), the films started to undergo substantial weight loss reflecting the rapid thermal degradation of GT and CS during this stage (Pan et al., 2020; Zhao et al., 2023).

After adding PVA, the thermal stability of both GT film and CS film was significantly improved, the weight loss of both films in the second weight loss stage was significantly decreased, and the maximum thermal loss temperature of GT film increased from 259.7 °C to 267.3 °C for GP film (Fig. 1E), which was mainly attributed to the higher thermal stability of PVA and the interaction between these materials. In the range of 50–250 °C, the thermal weight loss curve of GPC film shifted significantly upward compared to GP film and had higher weight retention after the whole heating process, suggesting that CDs further improved the thermal stability of the GP film. Overall, both GT-based and CS-based films showed good stability at the temperature below 200 °C, which was sufficient to cope with temperature variations in the food supply chain.

The surface micromorphology of CS-based films is shown in Fig. 2 . The CP film was relatively flat without obvious cracks and holes (Fig. 2A), in which the wrinkles and bumps might be related to the volatilization of water from the film and recrystallization of starch during the drying process (Shaikh, Haider, Ali, & Hasnain, 2019). After adding CEO Pickering emulsion, a large number of uniform bumps was observed in the CP-3%CEO film caused by emulsion droplet loading (Fig. 2B). Although the emulsion loading roughened the film surface, it did not significantly alter the continuity of the film structure. In contrast, the size of the bumps in the CP-5%CEO film was much larger, and significant holes appeared on the surface (Fig. 2C), which could be attributed to the aggregation of excessively high concentration of emulsion droplets during the film preparation process and the damage to the film structure due to the large amount of volatilization of CEO (Zhang et al., 2022).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Moisture content, total soluble mass, surface wettability
ce:section.ce:para: As exhibited in Table S1, gelatin-based films had higher moisture content than corn starch-based films, which might be related to the stronger water retention ability of gelatin than corn starch. Moreover, there was no significant difference in moisture content among films based on the same matrix, which might be caused by the same drying end point judgement criteria (the film was easily separated from the printing pad paper). The total soluble mass of GP film and GPC film had no significant difference. However, the total soluble mass of CP film decreased first and then increased with the increase of CEO content, and reached the lowest level when CEO content was 3%. Similar results were reported by Wu et al. (2023), who attributed the decrease in total soluble mass of the film to the enhanced water resistance caused by the strong interaction between the starch matrix and the appropriate concentration of essential oil.

It can be seen in Fig. S4, the water contact angle (WCA) of GP film and GPC film was 77.2 ± 2.0° and 77.6 ± 2.9°, respectively, indicating that CDs addition did not significantly change the surface wettability of GP film. The WCA of CP film was 73.0 ± 1.5°, which first increased with the rise of CEO concentration in the film and reached a maximum value (91.6 ± 3.0°) at a CEO addition of 3%. The WCA < 90° reflected a hydrophilic surface, and the WCA > 90° reflected a hydrophobic surface, which indicated that the surface of the CP-3%CEO film had already shown a hydrophobic state. The strongest hydrophobicity of the CP-3%CEO film could be caused by two reasons: On the one hand, the incorporation of the CEO with a strong hydrophobicity increased the overall hydrophobicity of the film; on the other hand, the CEO at the addition of 3% underwent strong hydrogen bonding with the CS, reducing the hydrophilic groups on the film surface (Zhang, Zhang, Mujumdar, Wang, & Ma, 2023). As the addition of CEO in the film further increased to 5%, the WCA of the film decreased to 75.5 ± 1.2° again. This was mainly due to the uneven dispersion of excess CEO in the film-forming material during the film preparation process, which not only intensified the roughness of the film surface, but also increased the porosity of the film (producing pores) (Fig. 2C) and in turn weakened the interaction between CS and PVA. Similar results were reported by Zhang et al. (2022), who found that excess oregano essential oil emulsions caused a decrease in the water contact angle of the konjac glucomannan films.

Thus, based on the aforementioned results, it could be confirmed that adding CEO Pickering emulsion into CS-PVA matrix with an appropriate content (3%, w/w) could significantly strengthen the water resistance of the resulting film, which was crucial to maintain the stability of the package and packaged food products.

The pictures and color parameters of the films are shown in Fig. S4 and Table S1. Both GP film and CP film were transparent with no obvious color. After incorporating CDs, the GPC film showed a brown color, with a significant decrease in L* value and a significant increase in a* and b* values compared to the GP film, which was attributed to the characteristic brown color of CDs. As the addition of CEO in the film increased from 0% to 5%, the L* and a* values of the CP film decreased from 85.20 and 0.81 to 83.39 and − 1.63, respectively, while the b* value increased from −2.35 to 5.86, indicating that the brightness of the CP film decreased slightly with the increase of CEO concentration, whereas the yellowness increased more obviously, which was in line with the change in visual color of the film (Fig. S4). Ahmad, Benjakul, Prodpran, and Agustini (2012) found that the color substance in the CEO was the main cause of the color change in the film.

The printed and dried bilayer films are shown in Fig. 3 . The GPC was extruded smoothly during the thermal printing process, and recovered to the solid state after printing, which made the surface of the printed film smooth and flat, and enabled to play a good supporting role for the subsequent deposition of CPC. The CPC had excellent printing accuracy and post-printing stability, and the printed lines did not have any obvious breakage and sticking phenomenon under different filling rates, which also well supported the results of the rheological characterization.

After drying, all the bilayer films maintained good morphology with no visible wrinkles or cracks. In addition, the two layers of bilayer films could be successfully separated, suggesting that the two layers were relatively independent of each other, and there was no fusion of the film materials, which is crucial for two layers to fulfill their respective roles. Moreover, the GPC film has high transparency, and as the filling rate of the CPC film increased, the transparency of the bilayer film decreased to some extent, but it was still high.

The overall thickness of different films and the porosity of the CPC layer (active layer) are demonstrated in Table S2. GP film had the lowest thickness (0.228 ± 0.009) and the addition of CDs did not significantly change its thickness. The overlying of CPC layer on the GPC layer significantly increased the overall thickness of the film. There was no significant change in the thickness of the bilayer film when the filling rate of the CPC layer increased from 20% to 60%. However, a significant increase in the thickness of the bilayer film occurred with the further increase in the filling rate (80–100%) of the CPC layer. The trend was similar to the results reported by Huang, Zhang, and Pattarapon (2022). The reasons for these changes might be: 1) the printing materials underwent a certain degree of compressive deformation under gravity at low filling rates, 2) the overlap rate between shell and infill was set to 15% to ensure the quality of the printed samples, and the neighboring filaments were extruded and fused at high filling rates, which led to the actual thickness of the film being greater than the designed thickness (Liu, Bhandari, Prakash, & Zhang, 2018).

Tensile strength (TS) and elongation at break (EB) were the main indexes for the determination of film mechanical properties and were used to evaluate the strength and toughness of the films. From Fig. 4A, there was no significant difference between the TS and EB of GPC film and GP film (p > 0.05), indicating that the addition of CDs did not significantly change the mechanical properties of GP film. The mechanical strength of GPC film was significantly increased by the overlying of the CPC film, and the TS and EB of the bilayer films were both increased gradually with the rise of the filling rate of the CPC film. It was not difficult to understand that the rise of the filling rate reduced the porosity of the CPC film (Table S2), the film matrix was more tightly connected with each other, thus being able to withstand stronger external forces.

Water vapor barrier capacity is considered to be an important index for evaluating the performance of packaging films, and high water vapor barrier capacity is beneficial for limiting the migration of water molecules, thus prolonging the shelf life of food products (Jiang et al., 2022). As shown in Table S2, after 24 h of equilibrium, the water vapor permeation amounts of GP film and GPC film were 0.0673 ± 0.0006 g and 0.0640 ± 0.0026 g, respectively. The smaller water vapor permeation amount of GPC film indicated its better water vapor barrier ability. The difference between the water vapor barrier capacity of two films might be due to the hydrogen bonding between the CDs and the film matrix, which made the free volume of the film matrix macromolecules decrease, and the difficulty of water molecules to penetrate through the film increase (Nie, Gong, Wang, & Meng, 2015). The water vapor permeation amount of GPC + CPC bilayer film decreased with the increase of the filling rate of CPC layer, and when the filling rate of CPC layer reached 60%, the water vapor permeation amount of bilayer film was significantly smaller than that of GPC film. The stronger water vapor barrier capacity of GPC + CPC-3 film, GPC + CPC-4 film, and GPC + CPC-5 film compared to CPC film could be attributed to their larger overall film thickness and lower porosity (Table S2). The overlying of CPC layer extended the transport path of water vapor in the film and prevented the diffusion of water molecules inside the film, and the CPC layer with denser structure (lower porosity) had a smaller water adsorption area, thus further giving the bilayer film a higher overall water vapor barrier capacity (Wang & Ajji, 2023).

Optical properties can reflect the ability of films to block UV light (Kowalczyk et al., 2021). Fig. 4B shows the transmittance of different films in the range of 200–800 nm. It could be found that in the UV region of 200–400 nm, all the films exhibited some degree of UV blocking ability, among which the GP film had the highest UV transmittance, which was 72.78% at 400 nm. The UV blocking ability of GP film was mainly attributed to the UV-absorbent components such as tyrosine, tryptophan, and phenylalanine contained in the film-forming matrix: gelatin. The incorporation of CDs drastically reduced the UV transmittance of GP film due to the strong UV-absorbing and reflecting capabilities of CDs (Zhao, 2022). The combined utility of above two materials resulted in the maximum UV transmittance of the GPC film in the entire UV range was only 28.55%, indicating that this film could be used as an ideal UV blocking film (barrier layer) to protect the CEO in the CPC film (active layer) and the packaged food products from UV irradiation damage.

It could also be noticed from Fig. 4B that the combination of the active layer and barrier layer further improved the overall UV blocking ability of the bilayer film. As the filling rate of the active layer rose to 100%, the maximum UV transmittance of the bilayer film dropped to only 5.50%. This further improvement in the UV blocking ability of the film could be attributed to the strong UV absorption of the CEO (maximum absorption wavelength of 282 nm) and the low porosity of active layer, which made it almost impossible for UV to pass through the bilayer film.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Release behavior of CEO from films
ce:section.ce:para: The release curves of CEO from different films are shown in Fig. 4C. At 0 d, the release rates of CEO from GPC + CPC-1 film, GPC + CPC-2 film, GPC + CPC-3 film, GPC + CPC-4 film, and GPC + CPC-5 film were 58.78%, 55.80%, 35.70%, 42.32%, and 47.96%, respectively, which were attributed to the volatilization loss of CEO during film drying process (Xu et al., 2018). It could be found that the CEO drying loss of the five films basically showed a decreasing and then increasing trend, indicating that CEO loss amount was significantly affected by the filling rate of the CPC layer. The high loss rates of CEO in the GPC + CPC-1 film and GPC + CPC-2 film could be attributed to the reason that the low filling rates (20% and 40%) of the CPC layer made it easy for CEO to be dissociated from the film and volatilized. The higher CEO loss rates of GPC + CPC-4 film and GPC + CPC-5 film compared to GPC + CPC-3 film might be because that the high filling rates of the CPC layer (80% and 100%) in the former two films made the water in the films less volatile, which led to a significant increase in the drying time of the films. By contrast, the good balance between the volatilization rate of CEO and the drying time might be the reason for the GPC + CPC-3 film to have the highest CEO retention after drying. The above results indicated that the adjustment of CPC layer porosity played an effective role in reducing the CEO loss. Additionally, the highest post-drying CEO retention of GPC + CPC-3 film might be related to the reason that, when the content of CEO in the CPC layer was 3%, CEO had the strongest interaction with the film matrix and the CPC layer has a dense microstructure (Fig. 2B).

During the storage process, the CEO in GPC + CPC-1 film and GPC + CPC-2 film was basically released completely at 9 d, while the CEO in GPC + CPC-3 film was released continuously for 15 d, showing a better slow-release effect of GPC + CPC-3 film. In contrast, the release of CEO from GPC + CPC-4 film and GPC + CPC-5 film was significantly slower, with only 82.97% and 81.22% release after 15 d, respectively. Additionally, the CEO in the CPC-3 film was rapidly released during storage, reaching 88.79% after 6 d, which was much higher than the 71.35% in the GPC + CPC-3 film at the same time. This finding was related to the rapid volatilization and degradation of CEO occurring on both sides of CPC-3 film without the blocking of the GPC layer, suggesting that the superposition of the GPC layer played a good effect of protecting and slowing down the release of CEO. Because of the highest post-drying CEO retention and good controlled-release effect during the storage process, GPC + CPC-3 film was confirmed to have a stronger potential for use in microbial inhibition and food preservation compared with other films.

The above results indicated that 3D printing method had the ability to improve the retention rate of CEO in the film drying process and control the CEO release through structural design, which is not achievable in solution casting method. Additionally, the drying time of the 3D printing-based bilayer films (3.2–4.5 h) in this study was much shorter than that of the solution casting-based films (12–20 h) with similar size (Wang et al., 2022; Zhao, Zhang, et al., 2023). It could be mainly attributed to two reasons, 1) the printing material of 3D printing-based film had a high solid content due to the need for good printing performance and supporting ability, while the solid content of the preparing material of solution casting-based film was usually low; 2) 3D printing could realize the one step-molding of bilayer film, but the solution casting process of bilayer film usually did not pour second layer solution until the first layer was close to dry. Although 3D printing method had the disadvantages of high dependence on the 3D printer and higher thickness of printed film over solution casting method, it was regarded as a promising active film preparation technology considering its outstanding advantages.

The antimicrobial ability of the films was determined via the disc volatilization assay, and the results are shown in Fig. 4D. The E. coli counts of the five GPC + CPC film groups were significantly less than those of the GPC film group, which suggested that the release of the CEO in the CPC layer exerted a good gas-phase antimicrobial effect. Among the five GPC + CPC films, the GPC + CPC-2 film, GPC + CPC-3 film, and GPC + CPC-4 film had relatively better antimicrobial effect, which might be because that they had higher CEO content and appropriate CEO release rates.

The results of radical scavenging assay for films, CDs, and CEO are shown in Fig. 4E and F. From Fig. 4E, GP film had no radical scavenging ability. After adding 0.5%CDs, the DPPH radical scavenging rate of the composite film (GPC) increased to 14.85%, and the ABTS+ radical scavenging rate increased dramatically to 77.68%. The difference in the scavenging ability of GPC film for two radicals was mainly due to the hydrophilicity of the GPC film (Fig. S4) and the high water solubility of CDs, which led to a faster and more abundant release of the CDs into the ABTS+ radical aqueous solution compared to the DPPH radical ethanol solution, thus producing a stronger ABTS+ radical scavenging ability.

In Fig. 4E, there was no significant difference in the DPPH radical scavenging ability of each bilayer film (GPC + CPC-1–5) and GPC film, which was mainly due to the reason that the DPPH radical scavenging ability of CDs was much stronger than that of CEO (about 90-fold stronger) (Fig. 4F). CDs were the dominant antioxidants of the films, and the additional antioxidant ability of CEO did not significantly increase the overall DPPH radical scavenging capacity of bilayer film. Moreover, the ABTS+ radical scavenging rate of the bilayer films showed a trend of constant and then gradually decreased with the increase of the filling rate of the CPC layer. This might be because that although the CPC layer (CEO layer) had ABTS+ radical scavenging ability, the CPC layer with a high filling rate (>60%) restricted the release of CDs from the GPC layer, which reduced the overall ABTS+ radical scavenging rate of the bilayer films. This result was similar to the finding reported by Zhang et al. (2023) that the chitosan outer film inhibited the release of clove essential oil in the inner pullulan film, which resulted in a decrease in the antioxidant capacity of the film. The above results demonstrated that the incorporation of CDs in the barrier layer substantially increased the overall antioxidant capacity of the film, which would effectively reduce the oxidative loss of CEO and food products.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application in the flavor retention of spicy essential oil microcapsules
ce:section.ce:para: The retention rates of the characteristic taste (sanshool and capsaicinoid), color (capsanthin), and aroma (linalool) substances in the spicy essential oil microcapsule powders during storage are shown in Fig. 5 . With the increase of storage time, the retention rates of four characteristic substances gradually decreased. Compared with unpackaged microcapsule powders, the retention rates of the characteristic substances in the microcapsule powders packaged in GP film decreased more slowly, illustrating the higher flavor stability of the latter. After 28 d storage, the retention rates of sanshool, capsaicinoid, capsanthin, and linalool in the microcapsule powders packaged in GP film were 67.24%, 86.58%, 53.38%, and 70.03%, respectively, which were much higher than those in unpackaged microcapsule powders (40.71%, 81.83%, 19.19%, and 56.99%). Furthermore, GPC film showed a stronger retention capacity for the characteristic substances than GP film, especially for capsaicinoid. It might be because that incorporating CDs significantly enhanced the antioxidant and UV-blocking abilities of GP film, which further delayed the oxidation of characteristic substances and also effectively inhibited the photochemical degradation of light-sensitive substances such as capsaicinoid.

After being packaged by the GPC + CPC film, the loss rate of characteristic substances was minimized. It was because that the further enhancement of the film functionalities made the characteristic substances less susceptible to losses such as oxidative degradation, photochemical degradation, isomerization, and volatilization. Overall, the 3D printed active films had an excellent flavor-keeping ability to spicy essential oil microcapsule powders, with the GPC + CPC bilayer film exerting the greatest effect.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application in the preservation of mangoes
ce:section.ce:para: Changes in the appearance of mangoes during 12 d storage at 25 ± 0.5 °C are shown in Fig. 6 . At 0 d, all mango samples had a bright golden-yellow appearance with no obvious browning or spoilage. After 2 d, the brown spots on the surface of the control group increased and the black spots appeared at 4 d. At the same time, the GP film group still maintained the initial state well, indicating that GP film played a role in slowing down the oxidation and rotting of mango, which was related to the effective isolation of external microorganisms and oxygen by the GP film (Yang et al., 2022). After the incorporation of CDs in the GP film, the UV-blocking and antioxidant capacities of the film (GPC film) were dramatically improved, which enabled the packaged mangoes to remain close to the initial state after 6 d. Thereafter, the GPC film group rapidly rotted and wrinkled, and was not different from the control group and GP group after 10 d. In contrast, GPC + CPC film group did not show substantial wrinkling throughout the storage period and maintained the initial state well up to 8 d. This was mainly due to the construction of the GPC + CPC bilayer film, which further enhanced the ability of film to block oxygen and UV rays as well as to reduce the penetration of water vapor (Table S2), thereby slowing down the oxidation and water loss of mangoes. Additionally, the slow release of CEO in the CPC layer formed a vapor-phase fumigation environment, which inhibited mango decay caused by microbial infestation. In conclusion, 3D printed active bilayer film also played an effective role in inhibiting mango spoilage and prolonging its shelf life.

In this study, PVA addition effectively improved the printing and film-forming properties of GT and CS, while the best addition was 6% and 7% (w/w), respectively. Using GT-PVA composite material (GP) and CS-PVA composite material (CP) as film matrix, CEO and banana peel CDs as functional substances, the active bilayer film (GPC + CPC film) prepared by 3D printing technology had good thermal stability, water resistance, UV barrier ability, antimicrobial and antioxidant activities. In which, CDs was the main source of the UV blocking and antioxidant functions, and CEO mainly provided the antimicrobial function. When the filling rate of CPC layer was 60%, the active bilayer film had the highest retention rate of CEO after drying and possessed good CEO slow-release properties during storage. Under room temperature storage, the developed active bilayer film had a good stabilizing effect on the sensory quality of spicy essential oil microcapsules. It also showed the great antimicrobial and antiseptic effects on mangoes, thereby keeping the fresh state of mangoes for 8 d, while the fresh state of unpackaged mangoes could only be kept for 2 d.

Kai Chen: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Formal analysis. Min Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Investigation. Bhesh Bhandari: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Investigation. Dewei Deng: Writing – review & editing, Investigation.
